item.title: Court orders K80 million for Gulf prison by December
item.description: A JUDGE has ordered the Government, the Gulf Governor and the two Gulf MPs to contribute K80 million to build a prison facility in the province by December. The facility should be able to accommodate 400 prisoners and 130 officers.
item.content:encoded:
A JUDGE has ordered the Government, the Gulf Governor and the two Gulf MPs to contribute K80 million to build a prison facility in the province by December.

The facility should be able to accommodate 400 prisoners and 130 officers.

An affidavit to that effect by Acting Correctional Services Commissioner Bernard Nepo was filed on May 11.

National Court judge Nicholas Miviri, presiding in the human rights case filed by Bernard Lukas and 75 other detainees from Kerema, Gulf last Monday, ordered that K80 million should be paid by the Government, Office of Gulf Governor Sir Christopher Haiveta, Office of Kerema Open Thomas Opa and Kikori MP Soroi Eoe before the end of this year.

“Each will divide the K80 million in equal parts to be paid in a lump sum to build the Gulf jail,” he said.

Lawyer Vincent Ngibe from the Office of Public Solicitor represented the 76 detainees.

Prisoners from Gulf are brought to Port Moresby for detention because there is no prison there.

Judge Miviri said the 76 prisoners remanded, detainees contained in the list provided “will be transferred to this new facility upon completion before 2026 calendar year ends”.

“Parties will file affidavits to prove the execution implementation of the orders,” he said.

He also ordered the upfront payment of K1.8 million for the acquisition of land at Mupa, Malalaua, Gulf.

The orders will be reviewed on Aug 10, Oct 12 and Dec 16 at the Kerema National Court.

Judge Miviri further ordered that Nepo should advise on the progress and development of the execution of the orders through office of the Solicitor-General George Akia.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Chamber explains challenges on subsidy
item.description: THE administration of the Government’s fuel relief subsidy programme has presented challenges, including delays in reimbursement and some uncertainty regarding its implementation, according to the PNG Chamber of Resources and Energy (PNG CORE). The chamber, representing fuel importers and suppliers, said it recognises Government’s efforts to shield the country from the effects of rising global fue
item.content:encoded:
THE administration of the Government’s fuel relief subsidy programme has presented challenges, including delays in reimbursement and some uncertainty regarding its implementation, according to the PNG Chamber of Resources and Energy (PNG CORE).

The chamber, representing fuel importers and suppliers, said it recognises Government’s efforts to shield the country from the effects of rising global fuel prices driven by geopolitical tensions and international supply disruptions. But it is concerned about the implementation of the programme.

PNG CORE in a statement said that while it acknowledged efforts through the fuel subsidy programme, industry participants had carried the subsidy costs in advance. And the timing of the reimbursements has contributed to uncertainty around the supply arrangements.

The chamber reaffirmed that it was not a supply availability issue. But continuing to supply fuel materially below cost, without timely reimbursement and certainty, places significant pressure on suppliers and creates unacceptable risks to ongoing fuel security.

“The decision reflects an effort to support the ongoing reliability of fuel supply across PNG while maintaining the financial viability of industry participants,” it said.

“Fuel importation involves significant upfront financial commitments, including foreign exchange requirements secured months in advance.”

Fuel prices in PNG are regulated by the Independent Consumer and Competition Commission (ICCC) and based on actual international market pricing, including Singapore benchmark fuel prices and applicable market premiums.

The ICCC announced substantial fuel price increases effective April 8 but the Government intervened through a fuel subsidy programme to reduce retail prices.

As a result, prices were maintained at March levels through this subsidy mechanism.

PNG CORE urged that fuel suppliers have no alternative but to now transition to market-based pricing, in line with the May Import Parity Price (IPP) and applicable product premiums, effective as of last week – May 15.

This will affect the ability of retail outlets and service stations to maintain continuous fuel supply.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Embassy hosts cultural day
item.description: THE Japanese Embassy in Papua New Guinea organised a Cultural Day at Vision City on Friday, bringing together art, tradition and cuisine to showcase Japan’s rich heritage. It was also to celebrate the resumption of Air Niugini’s direct flights from Port Moresby to Narita, Japan on July 18.
item.content:encoded:
THE Japanese Embassy in Papua New Guinea organised a Cultural Day at Vision City on Friday, bringing together art, tradition and cuisine to showcase Japan’s rich heritage.

It was also to celebrate the resumption of Air Niugini’s direct flights from Port Moresby to Narita, Japan on July 18.

Japanese Ambassador Hisanobu Mochizuki said Japan had been a close friend to Papua New Guinea.

“We started economic development assistance to Papua New Guinea even before independence, and it is over five decades now we have formulated a lot of projects in all provinces. Japan is playing a very important role and I am very proud of this partnership,” Mochizuki said.

Members of the public were invited to experience the elegance of Japanese paper art known as ‘origami’, learn the techniques of floral decoration using natural flowers in the Japanese style known as ‘ikebana’, and sample traditional dishes prepared by the ambassador’s chef.

Mochizuki said Japan offered scholarship programmes to Papua New Guinean students.

“Not only the Japanese Ministry of Education but also the Japan International corporation agency offers a lot of projects to young Papua New Guineans graduating in Japan with those scholarship programmes.

“We can contribute to future generations of Papua New Guinea who can play a very important role for the country,” Mochizuki said.

The event also featured a performance by the PNG Mini Sports Academy Karate Association to demonstrate the discipline and spirit of martial arts.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Opposition says K1bil fuel subsidy is constitutionally irregular
item.description: OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane has labelled Prime Minister James Marape’s K1 billion fuel subsidy scheme as constitutionally irregular. “Section 209 of the Constitution is unambiguous: no public money shall be expended except under, and in accordance with, a parliamentary appropriation,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane has labelled Prime Minister James Marape’s K1 billion fuel subsidy scheme as constitutionally irregular.

“Section 209 of the Constitution is unambiguous: no public money shall be expended except under, and in accordance with, a parliamentary appropriation,” he said.

Nomane said Marape disclosed on Saturday that K576 million was sitting in the Central Bank trust account.

But it has not been authorised by Parliament.

“This is not a bureaucratic failure, as the prime minister frames it,” Nomane said.

“It is the predictable consequence of operating outside the law.”

Nomane said the fiscal arithmetic was compelling.

He said, currently, import duty was eight per cent, excise levy (about 22t per liter), and Goods and Services Tax (GST) at 10 per cent collectively add an estimated 55t to 65t per liter to the pump price.

“At a retail price of approximately K4.20 per liter in Port Moresby, full tax removal would reduce the consumer price to approximately K3.55 to K3.65 per liter – a saving of over 13 percent without a single Kina leaving Treasury’s operational budget,” he said.

Nomane said that when fuel prices surged globally in 2022, Australia temporarily halved its fuel excise, delivering immediate relief at the bowser.

“Indonesia eliminates fuel taxes on subsidised grades entirely, anchoring social stability.

“Ireland reduced excise on petrol and diesel to cushion households during the European energy crisis.”

Nomane said the Opposition’s proposal was in the national interest and demonstrates orthodox, evidence-based fiscal responses used by responsible governments worldwide.

“Marape must take this advice instead of trying to reinvent the wheel and apply voodoo economics that only makes matters worse, whilst being ‘Mr Right’ and blaming the public service for his own incompetence,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Games breaches. Calls out for legal clarity of 2026, 2028 PNG Unity Games
item.description: A LEGALLY-binding agreement granting East Sepik the right to host the 9th PNG Unity Games in 2028 has been breached after the National Executive Council (NEC) shifted the venue to Southern Highlands and appointed the National Capital District to host this year’s national games. East Sepik Governor Allan Bird yesterday indicated he is seeking legal advice, stressing that “the contract is with PNG S
item.content:encoded:
A LEGALLY-binding agreement granting East Sepik the right to host the 9th PNG Unity Games in 2028 has been breached after the National Executive Council (NEC) shifted the venue to Southern Highlands and appointed the National Capital District to host this year’s national games.

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird yesterday indicated he is seeking legal advice, stressing that “the contract is with PNG Sports Foundation (PNGSF), not NEC. They (NEC) are not a party in the agreement”. It is understood that East Sepik had paid K2 million to secure and host the 2028 Games.

Bird added: “Now that they have deferred the games, our organisers will have to sit down with PNGSF and figure out a way forward.”

He explained that following the signing of the agreement earlier this year, organisers were permitted to apply for Public Investment Programme (PIP) funding, with funding for the 2028 Games expected from the 2027 National Budget.

Prime Minister and NEC chairman James Marape could not be reached for comment as he was en route to Paris. His media team advised that questions would be brought to his attention later this week.

Insiders familiar with the regulatory framework of national sporting events said the NEC’s move breaches the valid Host Contract Agreement with East Sepik. They criticised Sports Minister Kinoka Feo’s announcement on the 2026 and 2028 Games as lacking credibility and factual grounding.

Minister Feo at the weekend confirmed NEC’s decision that Port Moresby will host this year’s PNG Games and announced an independent technical and financial review of all PNG Games-related projects and contracts in Southern Highlands.

He said the audit will assess project implementation, contractor performance, infrastructure quality, financial acquittals, and value for money to ensure accountability.

Meanwhile, PNG Sports Foundation director of major events and PNG Games John Susuve said the Southern Highlands Host Organising Committee respected the cabinet’s decision but noted that “there is no gazetted NEC Paper forthcoming to warrant the deferral. Therefore, normal planning is continuing until formalities are complied with”.

The Provincial Executive Council will meet in Mendi today to discuss the status of the 8th PNG Games.

Susuve also noted that the situation surrounding East Sepik’s hosting rights and NEC’s deferral decision requires proper vetting and wider consultation.

“With mixed information floating around, we must reach an understanding that takes into account relevant measures. In all fairness, the NEC decision sits in well for all parties, but documented plans must guide the way forward,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Chiefs great motivator for local talent, says Johnston
item.description: ALEX Johnston believes the Papua New Guinea Chiefs’ entry into the National Rugby League (NRL) competition could open the door for more local players to reach top level of their rugby league career. “I know the talent they’ve got firsthand when I played with the Kumuls,” the South Sydney Rabbithos winger said.
item.content:encoded:
ALEX Johnston believes the Papua New Guinea Chiefs’ entry into the National Rugby League (NRL) competition could open the door for more local players to reach top level of their rugby league career.

“I know the talent they’ve got firsthand when I played with the Kumuls,” the South Sydney Rabbithos winger said.

“A number of those boys will definitely do well in the NRL system.”

Johnston also praised the work being done behind the scenes by Chiefs general manager (football) and chief executive officer, Michael Chammas, and Chief chair Lorna McPherson, as the club continues preparations for its entry in 2028.

The 31-year-old believes the biggest impact of the PNG team will be the inspiration it gives to young players growing up across the country.

“It will benefit kids growing up and seeing local boys getting a crack in the NRL,” he said.

“It gives them something to drive and strive for. It’s only going to be good for the game.”

Johnston also opened up on the personal side of his decision to relocate with his family as part of the PNG project, describing it as one of the biggest choices of his career.

He said discussions with his partner Taryn were important before making the final decision.

“She’s been unreal. It’s a tough ask moving countries. South Sydney has been my home for my whole junior career and 13 years in the NRL.”

Johnston admitted leaving the comfort of Australia was not an easy decision, but he believes the move will create new opportunities for both his family and the people of Papua New Guinea.

“It is a big move and the decision didn’t come lightly,” he said.

“But we’re happy to go over there and do what we have to do to help people, while also benefiting our family.”

Johnston said the experience of living in PNG will also help teach his children important life lessons.

“I think it’s going to be great to have some change and experience what life is like over there,” he said.

“Hopefully it gives the kids a sense of gratitude as well.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AJ’s Chiefs move has Bennett’s blessing
item.description: ALL-TIME National Rugby League (NRL) leading try scorer Alex Johnston has revealed South Sydney Rabbitohs coach Wayne Bennett fully supported his decision to join the PNG Chiefs; not just for football reasons, but for the future of his family as well. Johnston said Bennett was proud of the decision he made as he prepared for the next chapter of his career connected to Papua New Guinea’s historic N
item.content:encoded:
ALL-TIME National Rugby League (NRL) leading try scorer Alex Johnston has revealed South Sydney Rabbitohs coach Wayne Bennett fully supported his decision to join the PNG Chiefs; not just for football reasons, but for the future of his family as well.

Johnston said Bennett was proud of the decision he made as he prepared for the next chapter of his career connected to Papua New Guinea’s historic NRL expansion team.

“He told me he was proud that I made this decision for myself and my family,” Johnston revealed.

The comments once again highlight the strong bond between Johnston and Bennett, who has played a major role in the career of the PNG Kumuls representative.

Rabbitohs fans have long viewed Johnston as one of the club’s greatest finishers, with Bennett helping guide his rise into the NRL record books during their successful years together at Redfern.

Despite preparing to leave South Sydney after spending his entire junior and professional career with the Rabbitohs, Johnston, 31, made it clear his relationship with Bennett remained strong.

The veteran winger even hinted that a future reunion with Bennett may not be completely impossible.

“I don’t know if he’ll still be at Souths when I’m done,” Johnston said.

The remarks will only increase speculation around Johnston’s long-term future in rugby league, particularly with Papua New Guinea’s growing influence in the sport and the opportunities expected to come with the NRL’s 19th franchise.

While discussions around the PNG Chiefs continue to dominate headlines, Johnston insists his immediate focus remains firmly on helping South Sydney chase premiership success over the next two seasons (2026 and 2027).

The experienced winger still believes the Rabbitohs have the squad capable of challenging the NRL’s best teams.

Johnston’s confidence is significant given the timing of his upcoming departure, with the long-serving Rabbitohs star preparing to close one of the most successful club careers in South Sydney history.

“We know when we play the way we can play, we can beat any team,” Johnston said. “For now, I remain committed to helping South Sydney push for success before beginning my next journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: NCD women win U17 academy title
item.description: THE National Capital District (NCD) Under-17 women’s academy are three-time champions of the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNG RFL) Academy competition. NCD defeated Mt Hagen academy 18-12 on Sunday at the Santos National Football Stadium to win their third straight title since the academy competition started in 2024. The match was closely contested and went into extra time after the Wes
item.content:encoded:
THE National Capital District (NCD) Under-17 women’s academy are three-time champions of the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNG RFL) Academy competition. NCD defeated Mt Hagen academy 18-12 on Sunday at the Santos National Football Stadium to win their third straight title since the academy competition started in 2024.

The match was closely contested and went into extra time after the Western Highlanders fought back to take a slender lead before NCD leveled the scores at 12-12.

The final went into extra time and in the second five-minute period NCD surged deep into the Mt Hagen half before capping off a tense final with a well-taken try to defeat the Western Highlanders.

The victory continues NCD’s dominance in the women’s academy competition and highlights the growing talent pathway for young female rugby league players in PNG.

In the men’s U17 academy final, Mt Hagen edged Gulf academy 12-8 in a scrappy but physical contest.

Both teams defended strongly, but Mt Hagen managed to score the crucial points late in the game to secure the championship.

Meanwhile, week five of the National Youth Competition also produced several exciting results across the country.

In Pool A, Port Moresby Vipers and East New Britain Gurias played out a thrilling 10-10 draw at the Gelagele Community Sports Oval in Kokopo on Saturday.

At the Santos National Football Stadium, Gulf Isou defeated Central Dabaris 30-8 with a dominant performance.

In Pool B, Lae Tigers defeated Goroka Lahanis 18-10 on Sunday at the National Sports Institute in Goroka.

Enga Mioks also recorded an important 22-16 victory over Mt Hagen Eagles at Rebiamul Oval in Mt Hagen.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Push for DMO hydrocarbon products welcome
item.description: WE welcome Prime Minister James Marape’s push to reserve certain domestic market obligation (DMO) hydrocarbon products for energy production for Papua New Guinea. This is the clearest statement from the head of government that the government has the development of local energy products as a goal in the near future.
item.content:encoded:
WE welcome Prime Minister James Marape’s push to reserve certain domestic market obligation (DMO) hydrocarbon products for energy production for Papua New Guinea.

This is the clearest statement from the head of government that the government has the development of local energy products as a goal in the near future.

At his media conference before departure for France yesterday, Marape said he would meet up with Patrick Pouyanne, chairman and chief executive officer of TotalEnergies SE, the global giant which is developing Papua LNG.

In addition, to discussing the prospects of advancing Papua Liquified Natural Gas (LNG) to final investment decision (FID) the PM said he would raise the matter of domestic market obligation gas for PNG’s local energy needs.

Said Marape: We are aggregating our domestic market gas. That has been my strong push since 2019. Unfortunately,”, PNG LNG being our foundational project, all the gas is locked to sell out to our customers overseas but for Papua LNG we want to pick up five per cent of gas for domestic use.

“We are discussing on the price of the domestic gas. We do not want to peg it against international price…we do not want to be exposed to the price fluctuation. We are asking them to give us at the cost of production at the wellhead.”

The Prime Minister said the government will be seeking to obtain similar DMO gas from P’nyang LNG and from the proposed Pasca A LNG.

“We will draw up clear policy that domestic market obligation petroleum products will be made available,” he said.

The Mineral Resources Development Company will be working with the government and its investment partner in Pasca A to progress the Prime Minister’s policy.

“DMO gas has been high on our agenda. We are securing parcels of gas for domestic use,” the PM said.

Over the next six to seven years, he hopes to see the policy take effect and by the time PNG is 60, he hopes to see a fully energy secure PNG.

This is language we would have wanted to see in around the time of first oil in 1989.

At the time discussions were held about ownership of the oil pipeline from Kutubu oil fields down to the coast.

The pipeline ownership push was beaten back but the sense of became more and more obvious across the years as other oil fields were discovered, brought to production and had to be piped to join the Kutubu pipeline to Koki floating terminal in the Gulf of Papua.

There was no discussion about a domestic refinery, although a small diesel refinery was established which produced fuel sufficient for the project’s own production needs.

It would have required a government insistence to expand the project, possibly using the

No discussions were held between PNG and the Australian Government about the possibility of piping PNG gas to Queensland.

Within another decade, gas and condensate, were the big items on the agenda and still nobody was talking local refineries — not even the small modular formats that were useful in other hydrocarbon producing countries around the world.

Experts, both locals and expatriates, convinced government that PNG crude on its own did not have the mix that refineries needed and even if it did contain those ingredients, there were too many refineries in the region which made any competition from PNG seem uneconomical.

That is because people were on the export/import-mode. Nobody was thinking of developing the domestic energy for a growing and expanding industry.

Lack of cheap energy fed other long held views that it was way too expensive to develop local manufacturing industries.

And so other mineral and natural wealth of PNG was harvested and exported in its primary form. Gold, copper, and silver from Bougainville, Ok Tedi, Porgera, Lihir, Misima, Kainantu were exported in their raw form.

So were the fishes caught in PNG 200 mile exclusive economic zone and hard woods from PNG forests all over the country.

Cash crops produced and exported without any downstream processing.

Secondary industries, the heart of industrial development in other countries, was neglected in the country — no cheap source of energy for them.

It is time to cast off many of these long held views and develop a vibrant domestic energy sector because only its development can spur the development all other industries.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG Games failure must not happen again
item.description: THE PNG Games, as a concept and as an endeavour, is set to be rescued by the national government with the announcement by Sports Minister Kinoka Feo that a version of the popular multi-sport event will be staged at year’s end in Port Moresby. This is not the first time the national capital has been floated as an alternative venue to host given the preparedness of the designated host province.
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Games, as a concept and as an endeavour, is set to be rescued by the national government with the announcement by Sports Minister Kinoka Feo that a version of the popular multi-sport event will be staged at year’s end in Port Moresby.

This is not the first time the national capital has been floated as an alternative venue to host given the preparedness of the designated host province.

Organisers, the government included, persevered with it being staged in Southern Highlands until they finally released it was a not feasible or possible to have the games there.

The event gets to run its long-awaited eighth edition after several deferrals over the last eight years.

The Games were originally meant to be staged in SHP in 2019 but amount of preparation needed to bring the province up to standard to host a large sporting event was a task that would need significant funding and logistics to pull off.

This led to delays and push backs, deferrals and revisions of schedules.

The announcement by Feo is welcomed news for the sporting fraternity, every code and participant, who look forward to this biennial event will breathe a sigh of relief that finally something that was planned for, and for which they have been preparing for on and off over the last eight years will come to pass.

It could be that something else occurs that takes the Government’s focus away from the event, importantly from funding it and providing the necessary support to enable an event of this magnitude to come off.

At present, there are a some major national and international activities and situation that could very easily take all of the Government’s focus and through another spanner in the works of the PNG Games.

We hope that is not the case but in actuality it is very possible.

To be fair, this 8th PNG Games were impacted by the advent of the Coronoavirus (Covid-19) in 2019, the year the games were slated to take place, nixing any chance of the event taking place that year and its reverberations ensured the next 12 months (2020) were off the table as well.

This continued into 2021 and by then the Government was juggling other priorities, not the least of which was General Elections of 2022. There was also a national census that, like the PNG Games, had been put off for close to a decade.

One issue rolled into another and the Government’s ability to muster its resources and direct a concerted effort into running the PNG Games was inadequate at best.

There were simply other things that came up.

But this isn’t the first time a PNG Games has been hit by delays and reshuffling by the organisers.

The last games, 2017 in Kimbe, West New Britain, faced similar challenges and had to be shifted in a calendar year from the midway point to the final quarter.

There is a precedence but never for this long (eight years) and with the eventual abandonment of the Games being staged in Southern Highlands.

Apart from disappointing locals and sporting codes, the biggest concern that needs to be addressed is the amount of tax-payer money which was sunk into staging it in SHP.

To date, several hundred million kina was expanded for this event and while the money was slow in coming to organisers, it did arrive.

The amount of work completed in terms of venue construction and the what is left to complete is significant.

A story run by this paper recently, laid bare the fact that all venues were nowhere near ready to host the games in Southern Highlands this year and that more money would be needed to get them ready.

That is shocking because it is an example of the state not paying attention to a commitment and allowing things to stagnate and meander along with slowed progress until there are no options left but to pull the plug and write off the event, and the investment.

An audit of the funding allocated and its use over the course of the last eight years is in order.

Treasury, Department of National Planning, the Sports Ministry, Sports Foundation and the Games Organising Committee need to provide the detail that will give a clear picture as to how things went wrong, how much money was spent, what it was spent on and what is there to show for it.

This is a failure that must not be repeated.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urgent need for logistics, leadership
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea approaches a critical democratic moment with General Election 2027 (GE27), yet concerns about preparations cannot be ignored. Reports from the recent Electoral Commission workshop in Waigani highlight incomplete preparations, including an outdated common roll, unresolved security arrangements, unfinished polling schedules, and administrative delays.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea approaches a critical democratic moment with General Election 2027 (GE27), yet concerns about preparations cannot be ignored.

Reports from the recent Electoral Commission workshop in Waigani highlight incomplete preparations, including an outdated common roll, unresolved security arrangements, unfinished polling schedules, and administrative delays.

The nation still recalls challenges during the GE22 – violence, delayed declarations, disputed outcomes, and public frustration – which weakened confidence in the electoral process.

To avoid a repeat, the Government and Electoral Commission must act urgently, ensuring transparency, proper planning, and decisive leadership.

Citizens need assurance that funding, logistics, staffing, and security are coordinated well ahead of polling.

Stable leadership, including the confirmation of acting electoral commissioner Margaret Vagi, is crucial for credibility.

Security forces must remain politically neutral, focusing solely on law and order.

Urgent action now is essential to safeguard democracy, strengthen national stability, and maintain public trust.

Preparation delayed is democracy denied.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Shadow over judicial bench
item.description: THE recent handling of allegations against Papua New Guinea’s chief justice by the Ombudsman Commission has generated uncertainty and concern about the transparency of institutional processes. Initially, the chief justice was referred to the Acting Public Prosecutor over allegations of misconduct and was subsequently cleared.
item.content:encoded:
THE recent handling of allegations against Papua New Guinea’s chief justice by the Ombudsman Commission has generated uncertainty and concern about the transparency of institutional processes.

Initially, the chief justice was referred to the Acting Public Prosecutor over allegations of misconduct and was subsequently cleared.

However, the Ombudsman Commission later referred the matter again, this time to the National Executive Council.

This sequence of events has left citizens questioning the clarity, consistency, and accountability of procedures affecting the judiciary.

Confidence in institutions is crucial for national stability and development, and prolonged uncertainty can undermine public trust, both domestically and internationally.

For PNG’s governance and judiciary to maintain credibility, it is essential that authorities provide clear communication, ensure transparency, and act decisively to resolve outstanding issues.

The people of PNG deserve clarity and assurance that justice is being served, and that institutional integrity remains protected.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
